at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Filing fee
at_a_glance.detail: Varies by court and claim amount

at_a_glance.label: Court
at_a_glance.value: City Court or Justice of the Peace Court

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $5,000, you cannot split it into multiple claims to fit within the small claims limit. You must either waive the excess amount or file in a higher court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Louisiana allows you to file claims for breaches of contract and money owed up to $5,000 in small claims court. The statute of limitations varies depending on the type of contract.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 10-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend someone $2,000, and they failed to repay it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the agreement and any partial payments.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned a friend $1,500 with the understanding they'd repay in six months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from due date. Document the loan agreement and any communications regarding repayment.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $3,000 and failed to make the agreed payments.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from due date. Present the original promissory note and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided $2,500 worth of consulting services, invoiced the client, and they haven't paid.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from invoice date. Keep copies of the invoice, service agreement, and any correspondence.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $4,000 on their account with your business.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from last charge or payment. Maintain detailed account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $3,500 in unpaid rent over the past three months.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from due date. Provide the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for $1,200 worth of goods with a check that bounced.
claims.notes: 30-day notice required before filing. Include the dishonored check and notice sent to the issuer.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $25 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $4,500 loan, and the primary borrower defaulted, leaving you responsible.
claims.notes: 10-year deadline from default. Show the co-signed agreement and evidence of payments made on behalf of the borrower.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Louisiana's small claims court handles various consumer disputes up to $5,000, including issues with products and services. The statute of limitations varies by claim type.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,000 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to refund or replace it.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery of defect. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.
claims.damage_boost: Possible attorney fees and damages up to three times actual damages for knowing violations.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged you $800 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from service date. Document the service agreement and evidence of poor service.
claims.damage_boost: Possible attorney fees and damages up to three times actual damages for knowing violations.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory auto repair
claims.example: You paid $2,500 for car repairs, but the problem persists, and the mechanic refuses to fix it.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from repair date. Provide repair invoices and any correspondence with the mechanic.
claims.damage_boost: Possible attorney fees and damages up to three times actual damages for knowing violations.

claims.name: Contractor failed to complete work
claims.example: You hired a contractor for $4,000 to renovate your bathroom, but they abandoned the project halfway.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from breach. Present the contract, payment records, and evidence of incomplete work.
claims.damage_boost: Possible attorney fees and damages up to three times actual damages for knowing violations.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver goods
claims.example: You paid $1,200 for furniture that was never delivered, and the vendor is unresponsive.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from expected delivery date. Keep the purchase agreement and proof of payment.
claims.damage_boost: Possible attorney fees and damages up to three times actual damages for knowing violations.

claims.name: Unauthorized subscription charges
claims.example: A company continues to charge your credit card $50 monthly despite your cancellation request.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from unauthorized charge. Document cancellation attempts and bank statements.
claims.damage_boost: Possible attorney fees and damages up to three times actual damages for knowing violations.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $4,500 that broke down within a week, and the seller refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from purchase. Provide the bill of sale and any communication with the seller.
claims.damage_boost: Possible attorney fees and damages up to three times actual damages for knowing violations.

claims.name: Misleading advertising leading to loss
claims.example: A store advertised a product for $500, but charged you $700 at checkout without honoring the advertised price.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from transaction. Keep the advertisement and receipt.
claims.damage_boost: Possible attorney fees and damages up to three times actual damages for knowing violations.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Louisiana's small claims court addresses property damage claims up to $5,000. The statute of limitations is generally one year from the date of damage.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver hit your parked car, causing $3,000 in damages, and refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from accident date. Provide police reports, repair estimates, and photos.

claims.name: Neighbor's tree damaged your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell onto your fence during a storm, causing $2,500 in damages.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from damage date. Document the damage with photos and repair estimates.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A former tenant left $4,000 worth of damage to your rental unit beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery of damage. Present the lease agreement, photos, and repair bills.

claims.name: Neighbor's pet damaged your property
claims.example: A neighbor's dog dug up your garden, causing $1,500 in damages.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left your laptop with a repair shop, and they returned it with a cracked screen.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery of damage. Keep the service agreement and photos of the damage.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Louisiana's small claims court handles monetary disputes between landlords and tenants up to $5,000. Eviction cases are handled separately.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,200 security deposit without justification after you moved out.
claims.notes: 30-day deadline for landlord to return deposit. Provide the lease agreement and move-out inspection report.
claims.damage_boost: Possible damages up to $200 or actual damages, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Landlord failed to itemize deposit deductions
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $500 from your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: Landlord must provide itemized list within 30 days. Keep records of all communications.
claims.damage_boost: Possible damages up to $200 or actual damages, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Landlord failed to maintain habitable premises
claims.example: Your apartment had no running water for two weeks, and the landlord did not address the issue.
claims.notes: Landlord must maintain premises in a habitable condition. Document the issues and repair requests.

claims.name: Landlord wrongfully retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, your landlord kept your furniture valued at $3,000.
claims.notes: Landlord must return tenant's property. Provide inventory and proof of ownership.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your apartment.
claims.notes: Self-help evictions are illegal. Document the eviction and any damages incurred.

claims.name: Landlord failed to return prepaid rent
claims.example: You paid last month's rent in advance, but moved out early, and the landlord refused to refund the unused portion.
claims.notes: Landlord must return unearned rent. Provide the lease agreement and proof of payment.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant owes $4,500 in unpaid rent over the past three months.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from due date. Provide the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You paid $800 to fix a leaking roof after the landlord failed to address it, and deducted the cost from rent.
claims.notes: Tenant may repair and deduct if landlord fails to act. Keep repair receipts and communication records.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wages
claim_categories.blurb: Louisiana's small claims court addresses wage disputes up to $5,000. The statute of limitations for wage claims is generally three years.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 for hours worked but refuses to pay.
claims.notes: Employer must pay within 15 days of demand. Document hours worked and payment records.
claims.damage_boost: Possible penalty of 90 days' wages or full wages from demand until payment, whichever is less.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer failed to provide your final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: Employer must pay within 15 days of termination. Keep resignation letter and payroll records.
claims.damage_boost: Possible penalty of 90 days' wages or full wages from demand until payment, whichever is less.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in sales commissions, but your employer refuses to pay.
claims.notes: Employer must pay within 15 days of demand. Provide sales records and commission agreements.
claims.damage_boost: Possible penalty of 90 days' wages or full wages from demand until payment, whichever is less.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but were paid only your regular rate.
claims.notes: Employer must pay within 15 days of demand. Document hours worked and pay received.
claims.damage_boost: Possible penalty of 90 days' wages or full wages from demand until payment, whichever is less.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Louisiana's small claims court handles personal claims up to $5,000. The statute of limitations varies by claim type.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped and fell in a store, resulting in $4,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from injury date. Provide medical records and incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $1,500 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from demand for return. Document the loan agreement and demand for return.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: Someone stole your bicycle valued at $800, and you have evidence of their involvement.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from theft date. Provide police reports and evidence of ownership.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: Louisiana's small claims court addresses vehicle-related disputes up to $5,000. The statute of limitations varies by claim type.

claims.name: Dispute over vehicle repairs
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $2,000 for repairs that did not fix the issue.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from repair date. Provide repair invoices and any correspondence with the mechanic.
claims.damage_boost: Possible attorney fees and damages up to three times actual damages for knowing violations.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, resulting in $500 in fees.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from tow date. Keep towing receipts and photos of the parking area.
claims.damage_boost: Possible attorney fees and damages up to three times actual damages for knowing violations.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction cases are handled separately from small claims and may not be subject to the $5,000 limit.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: City Court or Justice of the Peace Court eviction proceedings

exclusions.explanation: Disputes involving ownership of real property are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Claims involving defamation are complex and exceed the scope of small claims court.
Professional malpractice claims require expert testimony and are not suitable for small claims court.
Matters such as divorce, child custody, and wills are handled by specialized courts.
Claims against the federal government or involving federal law are outside the jurisdiction of state small claims courts.
Small claims court does not handle class action lawsuits.
Small claims court cannot issue injunctions or orders for specific performance.
Claims for workers' compensation benefits are handled by specialized administrative courts.
Claims against deceased individuals must be filed against their estate in probate court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Louisiana does not allow punitive damages in small claims court. However, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases, such as bad checks or consumer protection violations.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $25 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.
Actual damages plus attorney fees and damages up to three times actual damages for knowing violations.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be recoverable in cases involving bad checks or consumer protection violations.

where_to_file.court_name: City Court or Justice of the Peace Court

filing_options: Any parish where one defendant resides
Parish where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: The defendant can request the court to transfer or dismiss the case if filed in the wrong venue.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: Availability of e-filing depends on the specific court; check with the local clerk's office.

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: Waiver covers filing fees and may cover service fees; check with the court.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: Waived fees may be added to the judgment against the losing party.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fee and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last charge or payment

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can facilitate settlement and demonstrate good faith.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: A 30-day written notice is required before filing a claim for a bad check.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: Employers must pay wages within 15 days of a written demand; failure to do so may result in penalties.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must return security deposits or provide an itemized list of deductions within 30 days.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: Claims under the Unfair Trade Practices Act require filing within one year of the violation.

forms.name: Statement of Claim
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's allegations and desired relief.

forms.name: Affidavit of Service
forms.description: Proof that the defendant was properly served with court documents.

forms.name: Answer
forms.description: The defendant's formal response to the plaintiff's claim.

forms.name: Counterclaim
forms.description: Filed by the defendant if they have a claim against the plaintiff.

forms.name: Motion to Vacate Judgment
forms.description: Request to set aside a default judgment.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Notifies the court and opposing party of intent to appeal the judgment.

forms.name: In Forma Pauperis Affidavit
forms.description: Request to waive court fees due to financial hardship.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or constable personally hands the papers to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail
methods.description: Court clerk sends documents via certified mail with return receipt.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A licensed individual delivers the documents to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent; if none, serve an officer or manager.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Louisiana's long-arm statute.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: May request court approval for alternative service methods, including publication.
service.avoiding_service_notes: Document attempts and seek court permission for alternative service.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendant should file an answer within 10 days of service; failure to do so may result in a default judgment.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant does not appear, the court may grant a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim even if the defendant defaults.
response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: Defendant may appeal the denial of a motion to vacate within 30 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If the counterclaim exceeds the small claims limit, the case may be transferred to a court of competent jurisdiction.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys but are not required to do so.
hearing.format: Bench trial with informal procedures; each side presents evidence and arguments.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Parties must arrange for their own interpreters; some courts may provide assistance upon request.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within 30 days
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some courts offer mediation services; check with the local court.

appeals.type_notes: The appeal is a new trial in the District Court.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment may be set aside by filing a motion to vacate; if denied, the denial can be appealed.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when debtor is W-2 employed at a stable job.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution, serve the bank, and the bank freezes and transfers funds to the court.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor has sufficient funds in the account.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: File the judgment with the parish clerk to create a lien; debtor cannot sell or refinance without satisfying the lien.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective for securing payment but may take time until property is sold.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell debtor's non-exempt property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ from the court, sheriff seizes property, and it is sold at auction.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns valuable non-exempt property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to revive the judgment before it expires; no additional fee required.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy may discharge the judgment; certain debts are non-dischargeable.
collection.priority_notes: First to record the judgment has priority among creditors.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can appear without an attorney in small claims court.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses must use their legal entity name; trade names should be registered.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors can send a designated employee with written authorization.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face challenges in enforcing contracts.

county_variations.detail: Parties are required to participate in the Online Dispute Resolution Pilot Project process.

pitfalls.title: Wrong defendant naming
pitfalls.what_happens: If you sue 'John's Garage' but the legal entity is 'JG Auto Repair LLC', the judgment may be unenforceable against the LLC's assets.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Search the Secretary of State business records before filing and use the exact registered name; for sole proprietors use the individual's full legal name.

pitfalls.title: Missed statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing one day after the deadline gets the case dismissed with prejudice.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Check the statute of limitations for your specific claim type before filing; for fraud, the discovery rule applies but be prepared to prove when you reasonably could have discovered it.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant isn't properly served, the case can be delayed or dismissed.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Louisiana's service rules carefully; consider using the sheriff for service to ensure compliance.

pitfalls.title: Filing in the wrong venue
pitfalls.what_happens: The defendant can request the court to transfer or dismiss the case if filed in the wrong venue.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: File in the correct parish based on the defendant's residence or where the cause of action arose.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant files a counterclaim exceeding the small claims limit, the case may be transferred to a higher court.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Be prepared to address potential counterclaims and understand the implications for your case.

pitfalls.title: Failing to collect judgment
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment doesn't guarantee payment; you must take steps to collect.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Familiarize yourself with collection methods like wage garnishment and property liens.

recent_changes.title: Online Dispute Resolution Pilot Project
recent_changes.description: East St. Tammany City Court implemented an Online Dispute Resolution Pilot Project requiring parties to participate in online dispute resolution processes.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in Louisiana small claims court. The process is designed to be accessible for individuals representing themselves, though you may choose to hire an attorney if you prefer.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The typical timeline for a small claims case in Louisiana ranges from 30 to 90 days from filing to resolution, depending on the court's schedule and complexity of the case.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: In Louisiana small claims court, you can sue for up to $5,000, excluding interest, court costs, and attorney fees.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear in court, the judge may grant a default judgment in your favor. However, you must still present evidence to support your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims judgment to the District Court within 15 days. The appeal is conducted as a new trial (trial de novo).

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Louisiana is valid for 10 years and can be renewed before it expires to extend its enforceability.

state_specific_notes: Louisiana allows for the recovery of attorney fees and treble damages in certain consumer protection cases.
Eviction cases in Justice of the Peace Courts are not subject to the $5,000 jurisdictional limit.
Small claims courts in Louisiana do not handle cases involving title to real estate.
The statute of limitations for personal injury claims in Louisiana is one year from the date of injury.
Louisiana's small claims courts do not permit jury trials; all cases are decided by a judge.

interesting_findings: Louisiana's small claims courts can grant equitable relief, including specific performance, in addition to monetary damages.
The Online Dispute Resolution Pilot Project in East St. Tammany City Court represents a significant modernization effort in Louisiana's small claims process.

sources.label: Louisiana Code of Civil Procedure Title V (City and Parish Courts)
La. Code Civ. Proc. art. 4831 et seq. (Small claims)
La. Rev. Stat. § 13:841 et seq. (Court fees)
La. Code Civ. Proc. art. 1231 et seq. (Citation and service)
La. Code Civ. Proc. art. 4924 (Small claims appeal)
La. Code Civ. Proc. Book V Title II (Execution)
La. Rev. Stat. § 51:1401 et seq. (Unfair Trade Practices Act)
